Full Comments:
Spoke with Eric, owner of four Cali Street Tire locations (operating under separate LLCs). Per management request, locations will be grouped together for tagging purposes. Important to note that their 5th location will be the biggest location to date, and visibly off the fwy. 
Eric requested an enrollment link, as he is planning to open an additional location in Corona (approximately 10 minutes from current shop).
Discussed competitor presence:
Eric confirmed that American First is still active and recently used (processed a transaction today).
Stated that Acima is currently his top financing partner due to offering a 3.5%–4% backend rebate.
Reported estimated combined monthly funding volume across all locations: $60K–$90K.
Eric indicated that the rebate is the primary driver of his decision-making; no other major value propositions were highlighted.
Attempted to gather more detailed insights, but call was limited due to shop activity.

Next Steps:

Explore potential rebate...

Subject: 00TTO00000t99kZ2AQ
Full Comments:
SW Michael, we went over this: 

1. They are on Warning List, they need to provide all invoices for review after customer signs.

2. Just because a customer was approved and signed, does not guarantee funding as we still need to review.

3. Once reviewed if any information does not recite or there are discrepancies found, we will request further docs from customer. This can also delay funding

4. For this reason, the merchant should not release the merchandise to the customer until we have informed them it has been approved for funding.

I tried to transfer him over to MS so they can go over a few apps specifically, but he didn't have time, wants a cb later today
_label_Full Comments:

I called Nadira regarding an email request about not receiving her spiff. The $25 spiff had expired in December. I explained that I could reactivate her at $15 based on usage, but she became upset and stated that Matt never informed her that the spiff was based on usage or funding, and that she was under the impression the $25 spiff was simply for using and signing up with us.
To resolve the situation, I placed her back on the $25 spiff through June 30th. I made it very clear that the spiff is based on usage and funding, and that we will reevaluate the spiff amount at that time.

Account/Contact: John – AAMCO (La Cienega)
Interaction Type: Follow-Up Visit

Summary:
Visited AAMCO on La Cienega and had the opportunity to speak with John. He shared that they currently use EasyPay as a secondary option, typically when customers indicate they may not qualify for traditional financing.

He noted that recently, most customers have been paying with cash or choosing not to move forward with repairs, which has limited financing opportunities.

Reinforced the value of EasyPay by explaining the 90-day same-as-cash option, including the $40 processing fee if carried the full term, and clarified that interest only applies to any remaining balance after the 90-day period. This positioning helps address affordability concerns and can prevent lost deals.

Key Insights:
• EasyPay used as secondary option
• Customers primarily paying cash or delaying repairs
• Opportunity to position financing earlier in the sales...

Subject: 00UTO00000YViAr2AL
Full Comments:
Checked in via Maps Application 
Training with Amir has been successfully completed. During the session, we reviewed the requirement that all transactions must be processed in person and that customer ID verification is mandatory. It was explicitly communicated that we cannot cover downpayments on any transaction. We also discussed that EasyPay will eventually audit shop invoices, so it is crucial that the invoices he writes accurately detail what EasyPay is covering and that the descriptions match our records. Amir acknowledged that we charge taxes on our end and stated this is perfectly fine with him. Furthermore, he expressed appreciation that we do not charge any merchant fees, noting that this is a strong advantage over SNAP, which charges him a 3% fee and currently serves as his default first option.  - Had Noemy create credentials for Amir to login. Made aware that he can create his profile and start using the program.

Subject: 00TTO00000t8Gpf2AE
Full Comments:
I spoke with Jay, and we had a very meaningful conversation. He explained that their process is to send customers a finance application link. We are presented as the first option, and if the customer is not approved with us, they are given three additional options: Snap, Acima, and Synchrony.
Jay mentioned that he’s not sure why they are not moving forward with our program, especially since customers are getting approved. He believes that many customers are either coming up with the funds on their own—likely due to it being tax season and having access to their tax refunds—or they are choosing to pay using their own credit or debit cards.

Reviewed alternative options, including business banking solutions, and he expressed interest in transitioning to a business account to better align with payment requirements.

Subject: 00UTO00000YTBR82AP
Full Comments:
Checked in via Maps Application 
Spoke with Lupe to review March activity and discuss the outlook for this month. We remain the exclusive financing option due to our no-merchant-fee structure, and she provided several new business referrals.

Meeting Notes: Lupe mentioned that March consisted predominantly of cash customers, resulting in lower financing volume. She anticipates having a few customers utilize financing this month, though it will likely only be one or two deals. We continue to hold our position as the only financing option at this location because they prefer our no-merchant-fee model. Before leaving, Lupe gave me a few solid leads, including referrals for other businesses in the same park and a golf cart shop located down the road, which I will add to my pipeline for future outreach.

Carlos indicated that financing has not been a strong fit for their current business model, largely due to the lower average transaction size. EasyPay has not been actively used as a result.

Next Steps:  
Focus on nurturing the relationship and rebuilding trust over time. Identify opportunities where financing could add value, particularly for higher-ticket items like electric bikes. Explore ways to gradually reintroduce EasyPay and position it as a solution for larger purchases to regain account engagement.

Subject: 00TTO00000t49tL2AQ
Full Comments:
Called and spoke with Alex. There appeared to be a slight language barrier, but I was able to discuss their recent applications. I noted that while there have been some approvals, we haven’t seen many fundings lately, and asked what might be preventing customers from finalizing. He mentioned that some customers are taking longer to decide, and for one recent application, they are waiting for funds to be deposited into the customer’s account.

I introduced myself as their account manager and encouraged him to reach out with any questions or concerns. I also followed up by emailing him my contact information.

Subject: 00TTO00000t4IwS2AU
Full Comments:
Alan shared that financing is not a focus at this location, as his customer base typically pays out of pocket. He does not actively promote financing options. When discussing shop potential, he emphasized that he prioritizes quality over quantity and declined to provide a car count, noting that volume is not a metric he tracks closely.

Based on the conversation, he appeared disengaged and not receptive to expanding financing efforts at this time. I also inquired about the sister location, Halsey’s Automotive. Alan confirmed that he manages both locations—one on-site and the other remotely—and stated that both are performing adequately. Noted that the credit culture at both locations is minimal to non-existent.

I let Alan know that I was his new rep and provided my direct line.

Spoke with Tanner, Vanessa & Richard, did some digging on the business and how things have been going with EasyPay. Subject: 00TTO00000t3xlU2AQ
Full Comments:
I spoke with Sammy, the store manager, and introduced myself as the Merchant Success Manager for the Easy Pay account. I informed him that we will be transitioning to a new business center for processing Easy Pay applications. At this time, there is no confirmed transition date, but I advised him to be on the lookout for emails from our team with additional details as the transition approaches.
During the conversation, Sammy mentioned that the store also works with SNAP alongside Easy Pay for secondary financing. However, he expressed concerns about SNAP due to a prior issue involving his Social Security number, where SNAP attempted to associate another account with his shop. He stated that this experience caused him to lose trust in SNAP, and he was not satisfied with how the situation was handled.
I sent Sammy my contact information and let him know that I will be his primary point of contact for any questions or issues that may arise moving forward.

_label_Account Name: Lucedan Inc. DBA Aamco Transmissions - Ina Rd
Subject: 00TTO00000t3zKD2AY
Full Comments:
I spoke with Jake, the Store Manager, and introduced myself as the Merchant Success Manager for the Easy Pay account. During our conversation, I informed Jake that we will be transitioning to a new business center for Easy Pay applications. I advised him to be on the lookout for upcoming emails related to this transition, noting that we do not yet have an exact timeline for when the change will take place.
I also provided Jake with my contact information.
Additionally, Jake shared that their store currently works with Synchrony, SNAP, and Easy Pay in the secondary finance market. He mentioned that the majority of their customers either come in pre-approved for SNAP or specifically ask for SNAP, which results in them submitting more applications through SNAP than through Easy Pay. This insight highlights an opportunity for improvement and an area I plan to focus on strengthening moving forward.

Stopped by the location previously known as Martinez Tire Repair and confirmed that the original owner is no longer affiliated with the business. The prior business is no longer in operation under that ownership.

This represents a new opportunity, as the existing account tied to Martinez Tire Repair is outdated and no longer applicable to the current business.

Key Insights:
• Original owner no longer affiliated with business
• New ownership in place for ~8 years
• Existing account is outdated
• Opportunity for new enrollment

Next Steps:
• Close or deactivate old Martinez Tire Repair account
• Initiate new enrollment for current business
• Follow up with Cesar to introduce EasyPay
• Provide onboarding support and marketing materials

Subject: 00TTO00000t3SOY2A2
Full Comments:
I called and spoke with Daryl and gathered details on their shop’s potential. He shared that their average repair order starts at $2,000+, they see about 20 vehicles per week, and currently operate with five bays.

After reviewing their recent activity, I noted they had one funded application in March and asked if business had started to pick up. He mentioned it has improved slightly. He also advised that they primarily work with Synchrony for their Mako credit card, which most customers prefer due to strong credit qualifications. He confirmed that many customers in their area have good credit and are typically approved through Integrity without issue.

I introduced myself as their account manager and encouraged him to reach out with any questions or concerns, as I’m always happy to assist.

I introduced myself as their account manager and encouraged him to reach out with any questions or concerns, as I’m always happy to assist.

Subject: 00UTO00000YSsQ52AL
Full Comments:
Checked in via Maps Application 
Met with Jay, Dallas, and AJ to update system access and discuss current financing strategies. Brought breakfast for the team to build rapport and encourage program usage.
Meeting Notes: Successfully created new logins for the team and removed old users who are no longer at the location. AJ is the District Manager overseeing the shop, while Dallas and Jay act as the service writers. Although Dallas and Jay are very familiar with EasyPay, the location's current process relies heavily on Formpiper as their primary financing option, which we are not integrated into.
Both service writers agreed to try pushing our program more, though they noted that recent customer declines have made it difficult to fully advocate for us right now. Bringing breakfast was a step toward overcoming this pushback and re-establishing our presence as a viable option for their customers

He shared that although they are a transmission-focused shop, most customers prefer to pay out of pocket or use personal credit cards, largely due to more favorable rates they already have established. He noted that customers are often hesitant when presented with higher interest rate options.

I reinforced the value of promoting our 90-day finance charge cap as a key selling point. While he acknowledged this, he mentioned that it has not generated much traction at this time. He appears to have a solid understanding of our product overall. There may be a slight language barrier, but he was able to address my questions effectively.

We discussed shop potential; however, from an opportunity standpoint, it appears limited at this time, as there is minimal credit culture in place.

Subject: 00TTO00000t3NP52AM
Full Comments:
Jasmine called in this morning regarding application #5154411 to confirm if she could proceed with finalizing the deal. She was unsure about releasing the vehicle since the estimated delivery date listed on the application was April 17th. I clarified that the date is only an estimate and that she can release the vehicle as long as the work has been completed and funding has been received. I also explained that she would only need to call in if the delivery date is exceeded and needs to be updated.

Additionally, she mentioned the customer was having trouble setting up a MyEasyPay account. I advised her to have the customer contact our customer service team. She confirmed that the contact number is included in his summary of terms, and I recommended he reach out if the issue continues.

Contact Observations:
Spoke with Itzel at a high-volume account. Timing of the visit was intentional at the beginning of the month to begin building trust and identify opportunities for revenue growth. Noted a strong presence of Snap Finance within the store. EasyPay is present but currently underutilized relative to the account’s potential.

Customer Feedback:
Itzel did not raise concerns but appears to rely heavily on Snap Finance. There is an opportunity to strengthen confidence and usage of EasyPay through additional engagement and support.

Next Steps:
Schedule a follow-up visit to expand EasyPay’s presence by providing additional marketing materials and reinforcing value propositions. Develop strategies to increase usage and compete more effectively against Snap Finance, with a focus on growing overall revenue through EasyPay.

Subject: 00UTO00000YQwCV2A1
Full Comments:
Checked in via Maps Application 
Met with Joe to discuss his current usage, address his concerns regarding our approval rates compared to SNAP, and provide a refresher on tax requirements.

Meeting Notes:

Account Status & Positioning: Joe is actively using our program and expressed a desire to use us more because of my local presence and support.

Competitor Intel & Pushback: He is frustrated that our approval amounts are consistently lower than SNAP's, and noted that we frequently decline customers who SNAP ends up approving.

Objection Handling: Acknowledged his concerns regarding the approval differences and assured him that we are aware of the issue and actively working on correcting it.

Training & Compliance: Reviewed the specific tax requirements associated with the EasyPay program, explaining the reasoning behind them based on our structure as a Lease-to-Own (LTO) product.

Subject: 00TTO00000t0Prl2AE
Full Comments:
I called and spoke with Santiago, who provided detailed insight into their current process. He mentioned that Pueblo Tires is no longer prioritizing EZPay. At this time, they are directing customers to the Pueblo Tires credit card first, and if they are not approved, they then refer them to Snap Finance.

He explained this shift is due to the spiffs previously offered through EZPay, which led service reps to promote EZPay over the Pueblo Tires credit card. As a result, Operations Manager Eric Valdez requested that employees stop leading with EZPay and instead prioritize offering the Pueblo Tires credit card.

Subject: 00UTO00000YQmrr2AD
Full Comments:
Checked in via Maps Application 
Account Visit – Rogers Collision Center

Met with Jonathan during visit. He shared that business has slowed down, with the majority of their current jobs coming from insurance claims rather than cash customers. This is a shift they implemented earlier this year.

Jonathan mentioned that many customers specifically request Snap financing. As a result, they present both EasyPay Finance and Snap as options, but Snap is often selected. He explained that they became a Snap affiliate after a customer preferred Snap over EasyPay, which led them to open a Snap account.

Positioned and reinforced the benefits of EasyPay Finance compared to Snap, emphasizing value and flexibility. Ordered additional EasyPay marketing materials to help improve in-store visibility and customer awareness. Provided business card for continued support and follow-up.

Noted that Katie had also connected with the merchant the day prior.

Full Comments: Miriam called in to inquire about application #80DAEE63 and whether it was a true decline. After confirming with the customer and reviewing the application chat, I verified that it was indeed a true decline. I then reviewed our requirements with her, including a minimum income of $750 and a bank account in good standing for at least 90 days. Full Comments:
I spoke with Carl, the manager, who recently completed training with Connor. He was very friendly, but it’s unclear how consistently he’s offering financing to customers.

During my visit, I printed and displayed the QR code in the waiting area and provided additional marketing materials. I also shared a few tips on how to better pitch financing. I’ll be ordering more materials for the shop and encouraged him to reach out if needed.

I reassured him that the updated platform is more user‑friendly, faster, and offers greater expandability, making it a significantly improved system overall.
I provided my contact information to Jacob and his team and encouraged them to reach out with any questions moving forward.

_label_Account Name: West Valley Tire & Auto Service DBA Big O Tires 4250
Subject: 00TTO00000szXsz2AE
Full Comments:
I spoke with Jacob, the store manager, and introduced myself as the Merchant Success Manager supporting their Easy Pay account. I also informed him that we will be transitioning to a new Business Center for Easy Pay applications and advised him to watch for upcoming emails, as we currently do not have an exact transition date.
During our conversation, Jacob mentioned that he was not aware that the store worked with Easy Pay. After reviewing the account history, it appears the store hasn’t processed any Easy Pay applications for the past five months, although they had been active prior to that. Jacob shared that he is not very familiar with financing—particularly secondary financing—and plans to speak with his district manager to gather more information about their previous involvement with Easy Pay.
I provided him with my contact information and will follow up to gather additional details and determine whether there are more locations associated with this account.

Despite this, she expressed that they like the program and view it as a strong option. She confirmed that the sales team is fully trained and aware of how to use it, so there are no concerns around knowledge or usage.

I informed her about the upcoming platform transition, verified the username on file, and let her know we expect the rollout to happen by the end of the month, though accounts are being migrated in waves. She appreciated the update and asked for a confirmed date, which is still pending.

Rachel requested marketing materials, and I confirmed the address for delivery. I also provided her with my direct contact information. Additionally, I will be sending a branch update to reflect their secondary location.
